     2вҶ’
     3вҶ’# Backgound
     4вҶ’
     5вҶ’## Background: ORCA

     7вҶ’ORCA: Observability with Real-time Control and Aggregation
     8вҶ’
     9вҶ’ORCA is a TBON-style, multi-tier observability + control runtime for BSP applications: MPI ranks emit telemetry into named, fixed-schema Arrow streams via collectors (AddRow), and at each timestep boundary the collectors are drained into per-timestep "timestep dataframes" that become the unit of batching, analysis, and persistence; ranks synchronously push dataframe metadata plus a bulk handle, and aggregators asynchronously pull the actual serialized Arrow buffers via RDMA GET from RDMA-registered memory, using a timestep-ordered priority queue plus tightly controlled RDMA-read concurrency to avoid buffering/OOM and throughput collapse. 
    10вҶ’
    11вҶ’On top of this data model, OrcaFlow provides a YAML-specified programming layer (SQL + optional map kernels) that compiles to tiered Apache DataFusion plans with operator pushdown toward MPI ranks and topology-aware sink placement, so ORCA can run in-situ reductions and/or persist (timestep, rank)-partitioned Parquet for fast post-mortems; a lightweight real-time path ingests aggregates into DuckDB at the controller and serves them via an embedded FlightSQL endpoint for live querying/dashboards. 
    12вҶ’
    13вҶ’The system is implemented as ~40K LOC split between C++ (overlay bootstrap, collectors, control plane, sinks, tier runtime) and Rust (OrcaFlow planner/execution on DataFusion), connected by zero-copy FFI so timestep dataframes are handed to Rust without copying and results are returned to C++ for forwarding/persistence; the overlay bootstraps with an initial TCP handshake then transitions to Mercury RPCs over libfabric (RDMA if available, TCP otherwise) and supports multiple topologies (e.g., DIRECT and MPIREP). Finally, ORCA's steerability comes from a timestep-consistent control plane (TS2PC: timestep-linked two-phase commit with О”-learning and tier "domains"), ensuring commands like probe/plan changes or tuning actions are acknowledged and then applied atomically at the same targeted timestep across ranks while remaining asynchronous to the application's collective domain.

file.content:
You are helping with a user study for an agentic workflow with an observability framework called ORCA.

# Backgound

## Background: ORCA

ORCA: Observability with Real-time Control and Aggregation

ORCA is a TBON-style, multi-tier observability + control runtime for BSP applications: MPI ranks emit telemetry into named, fixed-schema Arrow streams via collectors (AddRow), and at each timestep boundary the collectors are drained into per-timestep "timestep dataframes" that become the unit of batching, analysis, and persistence; ranks synchronously push dataframe metadata plus a bulk handle, and aggregators asynchronously pull the actual serialized Arrow buffers via RDMA GET from RDMA-registered memory, using a timestep-ordered priority queue plus tightly controlled RDMA-read concurrency to avoid buffering/OOM and throughput collapse. 

On top of this data model, OrcaFlow provides a YAML-specified programming layer (SQL + optional map kernels) that compiles to tiered Apache DataFusion plans with operator pushdown toward MPI ranks and topology-aware sink placement, so ORCA can run in-situ reductions and/or persist (timestep, rank)-partitioned Parquet for fast post-mortems; a lightweight real-time path ingests aggregates into DuckDB at the controller and serves them via an embedded FlightSQL endpoint for live querying/dashboards. 

The system is implemented as ~40K LOC split between C++ (overlay bootstrap, collectors, control plane, sinks, tier runtime) and Rust (OrcaFlow planner/execution on DataFusion), connected by zero-copy FFI so timestep dataframes are handed to Rust without copying and results are returned to C++ for forwarding/persistence; the overlay bootstraps with an initial TCP handshake then transitions to Mercury RPCs over libfabric (RDMA if available, TCP otherwise) and supports multiple topologies (e.g., DIRECT and MPIREP). Finally, ORCA's steerability comes from a timestep-consistent control plane (TS2PC: timestep-linked two-phase commit with О”-learning and tier "domains"), ensuring commands like probe/plan changes or tuning actions are acknowledged and then applied atomically at the same targeted timestep across ranks while remaining asynchronous to the application's collective domain.

## OrcaFlow Examples

Dir: /users/ankushj/repos/orca-workspace/orca-utils/orca-scripts/flows/
- kokkos_kernels.yaml
- mpiwait_onlycnt.yaml
- mpiwait_tracecnt.yaml

## Executing Commands

These variables should already be set in the environment (bash).

```bash
export PATH=/users/ankushj/repos/orca-workspace/orca-install/bin:$PATH
export ORCA_CTL_DASHHOST=h258.mon8.tablefs # TBON controller
```

Executing a command:
```bash
cmdrunner_main "<cmd>"
```

## Concepts

### Timestep Dataframe

A timestep dataframe contains all the data from a single BSP timestep (`ts`). If there are multiple schemas (MPI collectives, Kokkos etc), there will be one dataframe per schema.

The other relevant column is `swid` (sync window ID). Each collective call gets a monotonic swid. A timestep may contain multiple swids. swids divide all events into causally partitioned _sync windows_. Usually, you want to keep collecting MPI collective data regardless of the analysis, because it helps map swids to local time ranges etc.

You also want to preserve `ts` and `swid` in any aggregation flows you write. The flow is executed per-timestep, so you can assume that the referenced table there has only one `ts`, but many `swid`s.

### Controlling the application

The application runs a timestep in < 200ms. You want to pause it while you draft your flow, then resume it to run it. This allows you to debug errors etc. in peace.

You can also batch commands with 'cmd1; cmd2; cmd3' syntax. Do not do it initially, as you familiarize yourself with the workflow. Later on you may do it.

You can also do `pause; cmd; resume`, or just `cmd` without having to explicitly pause/resume. But do not do it unless user asks.

# Agentic Workflow

## Warm-up 1

Note that this is a complex systems research prototype. Not all log messages may be complete or helpful. Some details somewhere may be outdated. Use best judgement.

Execute each step after user confirmation.

Working directory for all artifacts: `/users/ankushj/llm-thinkspace/<YYYMMDD>-<AGENT>` (agent: codex/claude/gemini).

1. Run `status` to get info about the current application state
2. Application starts paused. Run `resume` to start execution, then run `pause`, and observe output.
3. Write a simple flow yaml to log per-probe counts (to see low/high-volume probes, also preserve ts and swid), emitted as parquet. Show to user.
4. Install it with `set-flow file <path/to/flow.yaml>`.
5. If it succeeds, run `resume` then `pause` back to back as separate commands. Note the timestep at which these commands take effect.
6. See if you can see the parquet output from the timestep ranges.
7. Disable unusually high volume probes with `disable-probe <schema name> <probe-name>` and capture counts again. The probes should not appear in the subsequent counts.

## Warm-up 2

We are interested in evaluating the performance of the code. It is an AMR code (Sedov Blast Wave 3D). AMR codes are known to have load imbalance problems. We want to characterize the nature of the problem.

All straggler problems manifest at synchronization points (collectives). Ranks that take less time at collectives are straggler ranks, and delay other ranks.

The first thing we want to do is understand the nature of collective durations. For each collective, if there's a gradual increase in the 0th %-ile, 1st, 10th, 50th %-ile durations: that is probably a load imbalance problem. If the 0th %-ile is 1ms and the 1st %-ile is 100ms, that indicates some tail behavior problem.

Execute a workflow to observe a few timesteps and then analyze their collective durations.

## Final Exercise

You are given a running AMR code that presents a performance anomaly. The anomaly appears on a few ranks at random and holds up the rest of them. It does not seem to be correlated with any particular rank or node, but appears randomly.

Your task is to infer as much as you can about the anomaly from the available data streams. Some points to keep in mind:

1. Collect `mpi_collectives` all the time. No need to use complicated percentiles. These will help you track the bad `swids`.

2. Remember that `swid` is a monotonic counter: it is a proxy for logical time. You can only collect more data for future `swids`, the simulation is progressing forward and you can not go back and collect data for past `swids`.

3. You want to collect as little data as possible, but do not use complex aggregations or joins. Use simple filters.

4. Remember that collective times are inversely proportional to stragglers. If one rank takes an extra 50ms, its collective duration will be zero and the others' collective time will be 50ms+. Lower collective times are the soruces of stragglers. You are not debugging elevated collective times but elevated times for events preceding the collective.

5. Remember that the anomalies you are looking for do not appear reliably. You can not assume that every timestep will have the anomaly or anything like that. Let the simulation run for a few hundred timesteps. Isolate interesting areas from a decent dataset. Then focus on the most unusual patterns.

6. Once you have some data, you can use this codebase to correlate application stats with what you see: `/l0/orcaroot/orca-umbrella/build/phoebus-prefix/src/phoebus/external/parthenon/src`

To query a stream, you can use this pattern:

```python
import polars as pl

stream_name = "mpi_collectives"
glob_patt = f"{parquet_root}/{stream_name}/**/*.parquet"
df = (
    pl.scan_parquet(glob_patt, parallel="columns")
    # more lazy filters here
    .collect()
)
```

content.text:
I've read the file. It's documentation for a user study involving **ORCA** (Observability with Real-time Control and Aggregation), a multi-tier observability framework for MPI/BSP applications.

## Key Points

**ORCA Overview:**
- Collects telemetry from MPI ranks into Arrow streams
- Uses timestep dataframes as the unit of batching/analysis
- OrcaFlow provides a YAML-based programming layer with SQL support
- Outputs to Parquet files for analysis

**Key Concepts:**
- `ts` = timestep, `swid` = sync window ID (monotonic counter for collectives)
- Commands run via `cmdrunner_main "<cmd>"`
- Application can be paused/resumed during debugging

**The Study Has 3 Exercises:**

1. **Warm-up 1**: Familiarization - run status/resume/pause, write a simple flow YAML to log per-probe counts, install it, view Parquet output, disable high-volume probes

2. **Warm-up 2**: Analyze MPI collective durations to characterize load imbalance (look at percentile distributions)

3. **Final Exercise**: Debug a performance anomaly in an AMR code (Sedov Blast Wave 3D) - identify random stragglers by correlating collective times with preceding events

**Working directory**: `/users/ankushj/llm-thinkspace/<YYYYMMDD>-<AGENT>`

**Example flows are at**: `/users/ankushj/repos/orca-workspace/orca-utils/orca-scripts/flows/`

Would you like me to start the workflow? I can begin with Warm-up 1 by running `status` to check the current application state.

content.thinking:
The pause command was successful. Interesting to see the TS2PC protocol in action:

1. First attempted to pause at ts=36, got PREPARE_NACK (ranks already moved past that timestep)
2. Retried at ts=37, still got PREPARE_NACK
3. Finally succeeded at ts=38

This shows the "delta learning" - the system learns how far ahead the ranks are moving and adjusts the target timestep accordingly.

The application ran from ts=-1 to ts=38 (about 38 timesteps) during the brief resume/pause window.

Now for Warm-up 1 Step 3: Write a simple flow YAML to log per-probe counts. Let me first look at the example flows to understand the format.

content.text:
Pause committed at **timestep 38**. The TS2PC protocol retried twice (ts=36 and ts=37 got NACK'd as ranks had already moved past) before successfully committing at ts=38.

**Summary**: Application ran ~38 timesteps between resume and pause.

---

**Warm-up 1 Step 3**: Write a simple flow YAML to log per-probe counts. Let me first look at the example flows to understand the format.

content.content:
1вҶ’nodes:
     2вҶ’  kokkos_filtered:
     3вҶ’    from: [kokkos_events]
     4вҶ’    sql: |
     5вҶ’      SELECT rank, SUM(dura_ns) as total_ns
     6вҶ’      FROM kokkos_events
     7вҶ’      WHERE probe_name IN ('region::Task_FillDerived', 'region::CalculateFluxes', 'region::TmunuSourceTerms')
     8вҶ’      GROUP BY rank
     9вҶ’
    10вҶ’  kokkos_ptiles:
    11вҶ’    from: [kokkos_filtered]
    12вҶ’    sql: |
    13вҶ’      SELECT
    14вҶ’        approx_percentile_cont(0.50) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY total_ns) as p50,
    15вҶ’        approx_percentile_cont(0.90) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY total_ns) as p90,
    16вҶ’        approx_percentile_cont(0.99) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY total_ns) as p99,
    17вҶ’        MIN(total_ns) as min_ns,
    18вҶ’        MAX(total_ns) as max_ns
    19вҶ’      FROM kokkos_filtered
    20вҶ’
    21вҶ’sinks:
    22вҶ’  kokkos_gf:
    23вҶ’    from: kokkos_ptiles
    24вҶ’    type: grafana
    25вҶ’  kokkos_pq:
    26вҶ’    from: kokkos_ptiles
    27вҶ’    type: parquet

file.content:
nodes:
  kokkos_filtered:
    from: [kokkos_events]
    sql: |
      SELECT rank, SUM(dura_ns) as total_ns
      FROM kokkos_events
      WHERE probe_name IN ('region::Task_FillDerived', 'region::CalculateFluxes', 'region::TmunuSourceTerms')
      GROUP BY rank

  kokkos_ptiles:
    from: [kokkos_filtered]
    sql: |
      SELECT
        approx_percentile_cont(0.50) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY total_ns) as p50,
        approx_percentile_cont(0.90) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY total_ns) as p90,
        approx_percentile_cont(0.99) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY total_ns) as p99,
        MIN(total_ns) as min_ns,
        MAX(total_ns) as max_ns
      FROM kokkos_filtered

sinks:
  kokkos_gf:
    from: kokkos_ptiles
    type: grafana
  kokkos_pq:

Since the flow is executed per-timestep (so ts is constant within a flow execution), I should group by probe_name and swid to get counts. Let me create a simple flow that counts all probes across all schemas.

nodes:
  mpi_coll_counts:
    from: [mpi_collectives]
    sql: |
      SELECT
        timestep as ts,
        swid,
        probe_name,
        COUNT(*) as cnt
      FROM mpi_collectives
      GROUP BY timestep, swid, probe_name

  kokkos_counts:
    from: [kokkos_events]

      SELECT

        swid,
        probe_name,

      FROM kokkos_events
      GROUP BY timestep, swid, probe_name

  mpi_msg_counts:
    from: [mpi_messages]
    sql: |
      SELECT
        timestep as ts,
        swid,
        probe_name,
        COUNT(*) as cnt
      FROM mpi_messages
      GROUP BY timestep, swid, probe_name

sinks:
  mpi_coll_counts_pq:

    type: parquet
  kokkos_counts_pq:
    from: kokkos_counts
    type: parquet
  mpi_msg_counts_pq:

content.text:
Here's the flow I created at `/users/ankushj/llm-thinkspace/20260115-claude/probe_counts.yaml`:

```yaml
nodes:
  mpi_coll_counts:
    from: [mpi_collectives]
    sql: |
      SELECT timestep as ts, swid, probe_name, COUNT(*) as cnt
      FROM mpi_collectives
      GROUP BY timestep, swid, probe_name

  kokkos_counts:
    from: [kokkos_events]
    sql: |
      SELECT timestep as ts, swid, probe_name, COUNT(*) as cnt
      FROM kokkos_events
      GROUP BY timestep, swid, probe_name

  mpi_msg_counts:
    from: [mpi_messages]
    sql: |
      SELECT timestep as ts, swid, probe_name, COUNT(*) as cnt
      FROM mpi_messages
      GROUP BY timestep, swid, probe_name

sinks:
  mpi_coll_counts_pq, kokkos_counts_pq, mpi_msg_counts_pq -> parquet
```

This counts events per `(ts, swid, probe_name)` for each schema and outputs to Parquet. Ready to install with `set-flow file`?

input.command:
cd /users/ankushj/llm-thinkspace/20260115-claude && python3 << 'EOF'
import polars as pl

parquet_root = "/mnt/ltio/orcajobs/suites/20260108/amr-agg4-r4096-n2000-run1/20_or_disable_paused/parquet"

# Check mpi_coll_counts
stream = "mpi_coll_counts"
glob_patt = f"{parquet_root}/{stream}/**/*.parquet"
df = pl.scan_parquet(glob_patt, parallel="columns").collect()
print(f"=== {stream} ===")
print(f"Shape: {df.shape}")
print(df.group_by("probe_name").agg(pl.col("cnt").sum()).sort("cnt", descending=True))

print("\n=== kokkos_counts ===")
stream = "kokkos_counts"
glob_patt = f"{parquet_root}/{stream}/**/*.parquet"
df = pl.scan_parquet(glob_patt, parallel="columns").collect()
print(f"Shape: {df.shape}")
print(df.group_by("probe_name").agg(pl.col("cnt").sum()).sort("cnt", descending=True))

print("\n=== mpi_msg_counts ===")
stream = "mpi_msg_counts"
glob_patt = f"{parquet_root}/{stream}/**/*.parquet"
df = pl.scan_parquet(glob_patt, parallel="columns").collect()
print(f"Shape: {df.shape}")
print(df.group_by("probe_name").agg(pl.col("cnt").sum()).sort("cnt", descending=True))
EOF

print(df.group_by("probe_name").agg(pl.col("cnt").sum()).sort("cnt", descending=True).head(10))
EOF

content.text: Ran ts 93-114. Now verifying both probes are gone.

input.command:
python3 << 'EOF'
import polars as pl

parquet_root = "/mnt/ltio/orcajobs/suites/20260108/amr-agg4-r4096-n2000-run1/20_or_disable_paused/parquet"

# Check kokkos_counts for ts >= 93 (after CheckAndUpdate disabled)
print("=== kokkos_counts (ts >= 93) ===")
stream = "kokkos_counts"
glob_patt = f"{parquet_root}/{stream}/**/*.parquet"
df = pl.scan_parquet(glob_patt, parallel="columns").filter(pl.col("ts") >= 93).collect()
print(f"Shape: {df.shape}")
has_checkandupdate = df.filter(pl.col("probe_name").str.contains("CheckAndUpdate"))
print(f"Rows with 'CheckAndUpdate': {has_checkandupdate.shape[0]}")
print("\nTop probes:")
print(df.group_by("probe_name").agg(pl.col("cnt").sum()).sort("cnt", descending=True).head(5))
EOF

content.thinking:
The user wants to skip Warm-up 2 and go straight to the Final Exercise. Let me re-read the Final Exercise requirements:

**Final Exercise:**
- Running AMR code with a performance anomaly
- Anomaly appears on a few ranks at random and holds up the rest
- Not correlated with any particular rank or node, appears randomly
- Task: Infer as much as possible about the anomaly from available data streams

**Key points:**
1. Collect `mpi_collectives` all the time - helps track bad `swid`s
2. `swid` is monotonic - proxy for logical time, can only collect future data
3. Collect as little data as possible, use simple filters (no complex aggregations/joins)
4. Collective times are inversely proportional to stragglers - lower collective times = straggler sources
5. Anomalies don't appear reliably - need to run for a few hundred timesteps, isolate interesting areas
6. Can use Parthenon source code to correlate application stats

Let me plan this investigation:

1. First, I need to set up a flow that collects mpi_collectives (to track swids and collective durations)
2. Run for a few hundred timesteps to get a decent dataset
3. Analyze the data to find anomalies - looking for:
   - Timesteps/swids where there's high variance in collective duration
   - Ranks with unusually low collective times (they are the stragglers)
4. Once I identify interesting swids, I can drill down to see what events preceded the collective

Let me start by setting up a simple flow and running for a while.

content.text:
Let's tackle the **Final Exercise** - debugging a random performance anomaly in the AMR code.

**Key points from the brief:**
- Anomaly appears randomly on a few ranks, holds up others
- Collective times are *inversely* proportional to stragglers (low collective time = straggler)
- Need to run ~100s of timesteps to catch anomalies
- Track `mpi_collectives` to identify bad `swid`s

Let me set up a flow to collect the necessary data and run for a while.

input.content:
nodes:
  # Always collect mpi_collectives - track swids and durations
  mpi_coll_raw:
    from: [mpi_collectives]
    sql: |
      SELECT timestep as ts, swid, rank, probe_name, dura_ns
      FROM mpi_collectives

sinks:
  mpi_coll_pq:
    from: mpi_coll_raw
    type: parquet

I have restarted the code. redo the actual exercise

    from: mpi_collectives
    type: parquet

content.thinking:
Paused at ts=264. So we have 264 timesteps of mpi_collectives data. Now let me analyze the data to find anomalies.

Key insight from the docs:
- Collective times are inversely proportional to stragglers
- Low collective time = straggler (rank that took extra time before the collective)
- Looking for: high variance in collective duration per swid, some ranks with very low dura_ns

Let me write an analysis script to:
1. Load the mpi_collectives data
2. For each swid, look at the distribution of collective durations
3. Find swids with high variance or extreme outliers (low dura_ns ranks = stragglers)
4. Identify which ranks are consistently stragglers

content.text: Collected **264 timesteps** of data. Now let me analyze for anomalies.

input.command:
python3 << 'EOF'
import polars as pl
import numpy as np

parquet_root = "/mnt/ltio/orcajobs/suites/20260108/amr-agg4-r4096-n2000-run1/20_or_disable_paused/parquet"

# Load mpi_collectives
stream = "mpi_collectives"
glob_patt = f"{parquet_root}/{stream}/**/*.parquet"
df = pl.scan_parquet(glob_patt, parallel="columns").collect()

print(f"Total rows: {df.shape[0]:,}")
print(f"Timestep range: {df['timestep'].min()} - {df['timestep'].max()}")
print(f"Unique swids: {df['swid'].n_unique()}")
print(f"Probes: {df['probe_name'].unique().to_list()}")

# For each swid, compute stats on dura_ns
# High max_dura with low min_dura = straggler behavior
swid_stats = df.group_by(["timestep", "swid", "probe_name"]).agg([
    pl.col("dura_ns").min().alias("min_dura"),
    pl.col("dura_ns").max().alias("max_dura"),
    pl.col("dura_ns").mean().alias("mean_dura"),
    pl.col("dura_ns").std().alias("std_dura"),
    pl.col("dura_ns").quantile(0.01).alias("p01_dura"),
    pl.col("dura_ns").quantile(0.50).alias("p50_dura"),
    pl.col("dura_ns").quantile(0.99).alias("p99_dura"),
]).with_columns([
    (pl.col("max_dura") - pl.col("min_dura")).alias("range_dura"),
    (pl.col("max_dura") / (pl.col("min_dura") + 1)).alias("ratio"),
])

print("\n=== SWIDs with highest duration range (potential stragglers) ===")
top_range = swid_stats.sort("range_dura", descending=True).head(20)
print(top_range.select(["timestep", "swid", "probe_name", "min_dura", "max_dura", "range_dura", "p01_dura", "p50_dura"]))
EOF

Interesting! I can see some swids with very high range in dura_ns:
- swid 1281 (ts 233): min=27us, max=95ms, range=95ms
- swid 1401 (ts 255): min=29us, max=95ms, range=95ms

This indicates stragglers! The rank with min_dura=27us was the straggler - it arrived late to the collective, so it spent almost no time waiting. Others waited up to 95ms.

Let me dig into these specific swids to find which ranks are the stragglers.

content.text:
Found significant anomalies. Some `MPI_Allreduce` calls have durations ranging from ~27Вөs to ~95ms - that's a 95ms spread where the low-duration ranks are the stragglers.

Let me identify which ranks are the stragglers in these anomalous swids.

input.command:
python3 << 'EOF'
import polars as pl

parquet_root = "/mnt/ltio/orcajobs/suites/20260108/amr-agg4-r4096-n2000-run1/20_or_disable_paused/parquet"

df = pl.scan_parquet(f"{parquet_root}/mpi_collectives/**/*.parquet", parallel="columns").collect()

# Look at the worst anomaly: swid 1281, ts 233
print("=== Straggler analysis: swid=1281 (ts=233, MPI_Allreduce) ===")
anomaly = df.filter((pl.col("swid") == 1281) & (pl.col("probe_name") == "MPI_Allreduce"))
print(f"Total ranks: {anomaly.shape[0]}")

# Sort by duration - stragglers have LOW duration
stragglers = anomaly.sort("dura_ns").head(20)
print("\nTop 20 stragglers (lowest dura_ns = arrived late):")
print(stragglers.select(["rank", "dura_ns"]))

# Check if stragglers cluster on specific nodes (ranks 0-127 on node 0, etc for 32 ranks/node)
straggler_ranks = stragglers["rank"].to_list()
print(f"\nStraggler ranks: {straggler_ranks}")

# Map to nodes (assuming 32 ranks per node, 4096/32 = 128 nodes)
nodes = [r // 32 for r in straggler_ranks]
print(f"Straggler nodes: {sorted(set(nodes))}")

# Check another anomaly
print("\n=== Straggler analysis: swid=1401 (ts=255, MPI_Allreduce) ===")
anomaly2 = df.filter((pl.col("swid") == 1401) & (pl.col("probe_name") == "MPI_Allreduce"))
stragglers2 = anomaly2.sort("dura_ns").head(20)
print("Top 20 stragglers:")
print(stragglers2.select(["rank", "dura_ns"]))
straggler_ranks2 = stragglers2["rank"].to_list()
print(f"\nStraggler ranks: {straggler_ranks2}")
nodes2 = [r // 32 for r in straggler_ranks2]
print(f"Straggler nodes: {sorted(set(nodes2))}")
EOF

input.command:
python3 << 'EOF'
import polars as pl
from collections import Counter

parquet_root = "/mnt/ltio/orcajobs/suites/20260108/amr-agg4-r4096-n2000-run1/20_or_disable_paused/parquet"
df = pl.scan_parquet(f"{parquet_root}/mpi_collectives/**/*.parquet", parallel="columns").collect()

# Get swids with high duration variance (anomalies)
swid_stats = df.filter(pl.col("probe_name") == "MPI_Allreduce").group_by(["timestep", "swid"]).agg([
    pl.col("dura_ns").min().alias("min_dura"),
    pl.col("dura_ns").max().alias("max_dura"),
]).with_columns([
    (pl.col("max_dura") - pl.col("min_dura")).alias("range_dura"),
])

# Focus on swids with range > 50ms (significant anomaly)
anomalous_swids = swid_stats.filter(pl.col("range_dura") > 50_000_000).select(["timestep", "swid"]).to_dicts()
print(f"Found {len(anomalous_swids)} anomalous swids (range > 50ms)")

# For each anomalous swid, find the top 5 stragglers
all_stragglers = []
for row in anomalous_swids:
    ts, swid = row["timestep"], row["swid"]
    subset = df.filter((pl.col("timestep") == ts) & (pl.col("swid") == swid) & (pl.col("probe_name") == "MPI_Allreduce"))
    top5 = subset.sort("dura_ns").head(5)["rank"].to_list()
    all_stragglers.extend(top5)

# Count how often each rank appears as straggler
rank_counts = Counter(all_stragglers)
print(f"\nTotal straggler appearances: {len(all_stragglers)}")
print(f"Unique ranks that appeared as straggler: {len(rank_counts)}")

print("\n=== Most frequent stragglers ===")
for rank, count in rank_counts.most_common(20):
    node = rank // 32
    print(f"  Rank {rank:4d} (node {node:3d}): {count} times")

# Check if stragglers are random or have patterns
print("\n=== Node-level analysis ===")
node_counts = Counter([r // 32 for r in all_stragglers])
print("Most frequent straggler nodes:")
for node, count in node_counts.most_common(10):
    print(f"  Node {node:3d}: {count} times")
EOF

The stragglers are fairly random - 185 unique ranks across 195 appearances. Some slight node clustering (node 103 appears 8 times) but not dramatic.

Let me look at the timing pattern - which swid within a timestep sees the anomaly?

input.command:
python3 << 'EOF'
import polars as pl

parquet_root = "/mnt/ltio/orcajobs/suites/20260108/amr-agg4-r4096-n2000-run1/20_or_disable_paused/parquet"
df = pl.scan_parquet(f"{parquet_root}/mpi_collectives/**/*.parquet", parallel="columns").collect()

# For each timestep, what's the swid range?
ts_swid_range = df.group_by("timestep").agg([
    pl.col("swid").min().alias("min_swid"),
    pl.col("swid").max().alias("max_swid"),
]).with_columns([
    (pl.col("max_swid") - pl.col("min_swid") + 1).alias("swids_per_ts"),
])
print("SWIDs per timestep (sample):")
print(ts_swid_range.sort("timestep").head(5))

# Get anomalous swids
swid_stats = df.filter(pl.col("probe_name") == "MPI_Allreduce").group_by(["timestep", "swid"]).agg([
    pl.col("dura_ns").min().alias("min_dura"),
    pl.col("dura_ns").max().alias("max_dura"),
]).with_columns([
    (pl.col("max_dura") - pl.col("min_dura")).alias("range_dura"),
])

# Which swid offset within the timestep has the anomaly?
anomalous = swid_stats.filter(pl.col("range_dura") > 50_000_000)
anomalous = anomalous.join(ts_swid_range, on="timestep").with_columns([
    (pl.col("swid") - pl.col("min_swid")).alias("swid_offset"),
])

print("\n=== Anomalous swids - position within timestep ===")
print(anomalous.select(["timestep", "swid", "swid_offset", "swids_per_ts", "range_dura"]).sort("timestep"))

print("\n=== swid_offset distribution for anomalies ===")
print(anomalous.group_by("swid_offset").len().sort("swid_offset"))
EOF

content.thinking:
Very interesting! The anomalies cluster at specific swid offsets:- swid_offset 6: 18 occurrences- swid_offset 7: 19 occurrences

This means the anomaly occurs at a specific point in the timestep's collective sequence - typically at swid 6 or 7 within the timestep. This is the 6th or 7th collective call in the timestep.

This is a strong pattern! Something happens before the 6th-7th collective in each timestep that causes some ranks to be delayed.

Now I need to understand what happens before these collectives. Let me:
1. Enable kokkos_events collection to see what kernels are running before the anomalous swid
2. Look at what the application does between swid 5/6 and swid 6/7

Let me first check what probes exist and which ones might be relevant. Then I can collect more targeted data.

content.text:
**Key finding!** Anomalies cluster at swid offset 6-7 within each timestep:
- swid_offset 6: 18 occurrences
- swid_offset 7: 19 occurrences

This means the anomaly happens at a specific point in the timestep's collective sequence - the 6th or 7th collective call. Something delays some ranks before reaching that point.

The user is right - I'm overcomplicating this. The ORCA framework has SQL support in the flow YAML. Instead of collecting raw data and analyzing offline, I should be writing smart flows that filter/aggregate the data in-situ.

The key insight from my analysis:
- Anomalies happen at swid offset 6-7 within timesteps
- Stragglers have LOW collective duration (they arrived late)
- I need to find what's happening BEFORE the anomalous collective

Instead of collecting all kokkos_events (which is massive), I should write a flow that:
1. Identifies the anomalous swids (high variance in collective duration)
2. Filters kokkos_events to only those swids on straggler ranks

But wait - the user said "there's literally no need to collect all the data". I should use the SQL capabilities to filter down to only the interesting data.

Let me think about what flow would help:
1. For mpi_collectives - I could filter to only keep rows where dura_ns is below some threshold (stragglers) or above some threshold (everyone else waiting)
2. For kokkos_events - I could filter to only keep events from specific swids that I know are problematic

Actually, since I already have mpi_collectives data and identified the anomalous swids, I could write a targeted flow to collect kokkos data only for those specific swids. But swids are monotonic and I can't go back in time...

So for future collection, I need a flow that identifies stragglers in real-time. Something like:
- Filter mpi_collectives to only rows with very low dura_ns (potential stragglers)
- Or compute per-swid stats and filter for high-variance swids

Let me write a smarter flow.

content.text: You're right - I should use the SQL capabilities to filter in-situ rather than collect everything. Let me write a smarter flow that identifies stragglers directly.

nodes:
  # Filter mpi_collectives to only potential stragglers (low dura_ns = arrived late)
  mpi_stragglers:
    from: [mpi_collectives]
    sql: |

      FROM mpi_collectives
      WHERE dura_ns < 1000000

sinks:
  mpisync_pq:
    from: mpi_collectives
    type: parquet
  stragglers_pq:
    from: mpi_stragglers
    type: parquet

input.command:
python3 << 'EOF'
import polars as pl

parquet_root = "/mnt/ltio/orcajobs/suites/20260108/amr-agg4-r4096-n2000-run1/20_or_disable_paused/parquet"
df = pl.scan_parquet(f"{parquet_root}/mpi_collectives/**/*.parquet", parallel="columns").collect()

# Get swid offset within each timestep
ts_base = df.group_by("timestep").agg(pl.col("swid").min().alias("base_swid"))
df = df.join(ts_base, on="timestep").with_columns([
    (pl.col("swid") - pl.col("base_swid")).alias("swid_offset")
])

# What collective types appear at each swid offset?
print("=== Collective type by swid_offset ===")
offset_probes = df.group_by(["swid_offset", "probe_name"]).len().sort(["swid_offset", "probe_name"])
print(offset_probes.filter(pl.col("swid_offset") <= 10))

# Duration stats by swid_offset for MPI_Allreduce (where anomalies occur)
print("\n=== MPI_Allreduce duration stats by swid_offset ===")
allreduce_stats = df.filter(pl.col("probe_name") == "MPI_Allreduce").group_by("swid_offset").agg([
    pl.col("dura_ns").min().alias("min"),
    pl.col("dura_ns").quantile(0.01).alias("p01"),
    pl.col("dura_ns").quantile(0.50).alias("p50"),
    pl.col("dura_ns").max().alias("max"),
    (pl.col("dura_ns").max() - pl.col("dura_ns").min()).alias("range"),
]).sort("swid_offset")
print(allreduce_stats)
EOF

- swid_offset 3, 4: MPI_Allreduce with low range (~240Вөs, ~2.8ms) - normal- swid_offset 6, 7: MPI_Allreduce with range 93-95ms - **highest variance!**- swid_offset 15, 20: Also some high variance (but rare)The pattern at swid_offset 0, 6, 7 shows very high variance (88-95ms range), while offset 3, 4 are normal (sub-1ms range).So the anomaly specifically affects MPI_Allreduce calls at offsets 0, 6, 7. What happens before those collectives that causes such variance?Let me look at what the sequence is:- offset 0: MPI_Allreduce, MPI_Bcast- offset 1: MPI_Allgather, MPI_Bcast
- offset 2: MPI_Allgather
- ...
- offset 6: MPI_Allreduce (high variance!)- offset 7: MPI_Allgatherv, MPI_Allreduce (high variance!)This is likely related to some computation or communication that happens between offsets 4 and 6. Now I need to look at what kokkos kernels run in that interval.

message.content: also filtering out high mpi collective times is silly. you only know that rank x was a straggler because rank y took a lot of time for that collective. don't try to filter the collective stream. recall the probe counts.t hat stream costs nothing. it's the other streams where you ened to optimize there

content.thinking:
The user is right. I'm overcomplicating this. Key points:

1. mpi_collectives is cheap - keep collecting all of it
2. The expensive streams are kokkos_events and mpi_messages
3. I need to filter those expensive streams, not mpi_collectives
4. I already identified the pattern: anomalies at swid_offset 6-7

So the strategy should be:
1. Keep collecting all mpi_collectives (cheap, helps track swids)
2. Filter kokkos_events to only events at swid 6 or 7 (where anomalies occur)
3. Or filter kokkos_events to only long-duration events

Let me write a flow that:
- Collects all mpi_collectives
- Filters kokkos_events to only swid that match the anomalous pattern (offset 6-7)

But wait - I don't know the swid offset in the flow SQL since I'd need to join with per-timestep min swid. That's complex.

Simpler approach: filter kokkos_events to only events with high dura_ns (the slow ones that might be causing stragglers). Or filter to specific probe_names that are likely culprits.

Let me think about what would cause a straggler at swid 6-7. The straggler rank did something slow before reaching the collective. So on that rank, there would be kokkos events with high duration right before swid 6-7.

I could filter kokkos_events to:
- Only events with high dura_ns (say > 10ms)
- Only events at swid 5, 6, 7 (around the anomalous collectives)

Let me try filtering by swid range or by duration.

content.text:
You're right. mpi_collectives is cheap - I should keep all of it to track swids. The expensive stream is kokkos_events. Let me filter that intelligently.

Since anomalies occur at swid offset 6-7, I should filter kokkos_events to only those swids, or filter to only high-duration events that could be causing the straggler.

input.content:
nodes:
  # Filter kokkos to only slow events (potential straggler causes)
  kokkos_slow:
    from: [kokkos_events]
    sql: |
      SELECT timestep, swid, rank, probe_name, dura_ns
      FROM kokkos_events
      WHERE dura_ns > 10000000

sinks:
  mpisync_pq:
    from: mpi_collectives
    type: parquet
  kokkos_slow_pq:
    from: kokkos_slow
    type: parquet

input.command:
python3 << 'EOF'
import polars as pl

parquet_root = "/mnt/ltio/orcajobs/suites/20260108/amr-agg4-r4096-n2000-run1/20_or_disable_paused/parquet"

# Check if kokkos_slow exists
import os
kokkos_slow_path = f"{parquet_root}/kokkos_slow"
if os.path.exists(kokkos_slow_path):
    ks = pl.scan_parquet(f"{kokkos_slow_path}/**/*.parquet", parallel="columns").collect()
    print(f"kokkos_slow rows: {ks.shape[0]}")
    if ks.shape[0] > 0:
        print(f"Timestep range: {ks['timestep'].min()} - {ks['timestep'].max()}")
        print("\n=== Slow kokkos probes ===")
        print(ks.group_by("probe_name").agg([
            pl.len().alias("count"),
            pl.col("dura_ns").mean().alias("mean_dura_ms"),
        ]).with_columns(pl.col("mean_dura_ms") / 1e6).sort("count", descending=True).head(20))
else:
    print("kokkos_slow not found yet")

# Also check what directories exist
print("\n=== Available parquet dirs ===")
for d in sorted(os.listdir(parquet_root)):
    print(f"  {d}")
EOF

Let me correlate these with the straggler ranks at anomalous swids.

input.command:
python3 << 'EOF'
import polars as pl

parquet_root = "/mnt/ltio/orcajobs/suites/20260108/amr-agg4-r4096-n2000-run1/20_or_disable_paused/parquet"

# Load data
mpi = pl.scan_parquet(f"{parquet_root}/mpi_collectives/**/*.parquet").collect()
ks = pl.scan_parquet(f"{parquet_root}/kokkos_slow/**/*.parquet").collect()

# Filter to new timestep range
mpi = mpi.filter(pl.col("timestep") >= 265)
print(f"MPI collectives: {mpi.shape[0]} rows, ts {mpi['timestep'].min()}-{mpi['timestep'].max()}")

# Find anomalous swids (high variance MPI_Allreduce)
swid_stats = mpi.filter(pl.col("probe_name") == "MPI_Allreduce").group_by(["timestep", "swid"]).agg([
    pl.col("dura_ns").min().alias("min_dura"),
    pl.col("dura_ns").max().alias("max_dura"),
]).with_columns((pl.col("max_dura") - pl.col("min_dura")).alias("range_dura"))

anomalous = swid_stats.filter(pl.col("range_dura") > 50_000_000)
print(f"\nAnomalous swids (range > 50ms): {anomalous.shape[0]}")

# Pick one anomalous swid to investigate
if anomalous.shape[0] > 0:
    sample = anomalous.sort("range_dura", descending=True).head(1).to_dicts()[0]
    ts, swid = sample["timestep"], sample["swid"]
    print(f"\nInvestigating: ts={ts}, swid={swid}, range={sample['range_dura']/1e6:.1f}ms")
    
    # Find straggler ranks (low dura_ns)
    this_coll = mpi.filter((pl.col("timestep") == ts) & (pl.col("swid") == swid) & (pl.col("probe_name") == "MPI_Allreduce"))
    stragglers = this_coll.sort("dura_ns").head(10)
    straggler_ranks = stragglers["rank"].to_list()
    print(f"Top straggler ranks: {straggler_ranks}")
    
    # What swid do the slow kokkos events occur at for these ranks in this timestep?
    ks_this_ts = ks.filter((pl.col("timestep") == ts) & (pl.col("rank").is_in(straggler_ranks)))
    print(f"\nSlow kokkos events on straggler ranks in ts={ts}:")
    print(ks_this_ts.group_by(["swid", "probe_name"]).agg([
        pl.len().alias("count"),
        pl.col("dura_ns").sum().alias("total_ns"),
    ]).with_columns((pl.col("total_ns")/1e6).alias("total_ms")).sort("swid"))
EOF

  - swid 1875: `LoadBalancingAndAdaptiveMeshRefinement` (1.2 seconds total!), `UpdateMeshBlockTree` (88ms)

The straggler ranks are doing mesh refinement operations that take over a second, which causes them to arrive late to the MPI_Allreduce collective.The anomaly pattern:1. Load balancing detects need for mesh refinement2. Some ranks get heavy redistribution work3. Those ranks do `RedistributeAndRefineMeshBlocks` (~1 second)4. Then `Mesh::Initialize` (~1 second)5. Finally they arrive at the MPI_Allreduce, but everyone else has been waiting

The question is: why do only some ranks get this heavy work? Let me check if certain ranks consistently get the heavy load.

input.command:
python3 << 'EOF'
import polars as pl
from collections import Counter

parquet_root = "/mnt/ltio/orcajobs/suites/20260108/amr-agg4-r4096-n2000-run1/20_or_disable_paused/parquet"

ks = pl.scan_parquet(f"{parquet_root}/kokkos_slow/**/*.parquet").collect()

# Focus on the heaviest operation: RedistributeAndRefineMeshBlocks
redistribute = ks.filter(pl.col("probe_name").str.contains("RedistributeAndRefine"))
print(f"RedistributeAndRefineMeshBlocks events: {redistribute.shape[0]}")
print(f"Duration range: {redistribute['dura_ns'].min()/1e6:.1f}ms - {redistribute['dura_ns'].max()/1e6:.1f}ms")

# Which ranks get this work?
rank_counts = redistribute.group_by("rank").len().sort("len", descending=True)
print(f"\nUnique ranks affected: {rank_counts.shape[0]} / 4096")
print("\nTop 20 ranks by RedistributeAndRefine frequency:")
print(rank_counts.head(20))

# Is there a pattern? Check if same ranks appear across timesteps
print("\n=== Per-timestep rank distribution ===")
ts_rank = redistribute.group_by(["timestep"]).agg([
    pl.col("rank").n_unique().alias("unique_ranks"),
    pl.col("rank").min().alias("min_rank"),
    pl.col("rank").max().alias("max_rank"),
])
print(ts_rank.sort("timestep").head(10))

# Check if the affected ranks cluster together
print("\n=== Node distribution of affected ranks ===")
affected_ranks = redistribute["rank"].unique().to_list()
nodes = [r // 32 for r in affected_ranks]
node_counts = Counter(nodes)
print(f"Total affected nodes: {len(node_counts)} / 128")
print("Top nodes:", node_counts.most_common(10))
EOF

content.thinking:
Interesting! All 4096 ranks get `RedistributeAndRefineMeshBlocks` work every timestep. So it's not about *which* ranks do the work - all ranks do it. The question is: why do some ranks take much longer?

The duration range is 18.6ms - 112.3ms. So the max is 6x slower than the min. This variance is what causes stragglers.

Let me look at the duration distribution more carefully and see what differs between fast and slow ranks.

content.text: All 4096 ranks do `RedistributeAndRefineMeshBlocks` - but duration varies 18ms-112ms. Let me see what differs between fast and slow executions.

input.command:
python3 << 'EOF'
import polars as pl

parquet_root = "/mnt/ltio/orcajobs/suites/20260108/amr-agg4-r4096-n2000-run1/20_or_disable_paused/parquet"

ks = pl.scan_parquet(f"{parquet_root}/kokkos_slow/**/*.parquet").collect()

# Focus on RedistributeAndRefineMeshBlocks
redistribute = ks.filter(pl.col("probe_name").str.contains("RedistributeAndRefine"))

# Per-timestep: check variance
ts_stats = redistribute.group_by("timestep").agg([
    pl.col("dura_ns").min().alias("min_ns"),
    pl.col("dura_ns").max().alias("max_ns"),
    pl.col("dura_ns").mean().alias("mean_ns"),
    pl.col("dura_ns").std().alias("std_ns"),
]).with_columns([
    (pl.col("max_ns") - pl.col("min_ns")).alias("range_ns"),
    (pl.col("max_ns") / pl.col("min_ns")).alias("ratio"),
]).sort("range_ns", descending=True)

print("=== Per-timestep duration variance for RedistributeAndRefine ===")
print(ts_stats.select(["timestep", "min_ns", "max_ns", "range_ns", "ratio"]).with_columns([
    (pl.col("min_ns")/1e6).alias("min_ms"),
    (pl.col("max_ns")/1e6).alias("max_ms"),
    (pl.col("range_ns")/1e6).alias("range_ms"),
]).select(["timestep", "min_ms", "max_ms", "range_ms", "ratio"]).head(10))

# For timestep with highest variance, which ranks are slow?
worst_ts = ts_stats.head(1)["timestep"].to_list()[0]
print(f"\n=== Worst timestep: {worst_ts} ===")
worst = redistribute.filter(pl.col("timestep") == worst_ts).sort("dura_ns", descending=True)
print("Slowest 20 ranks:")
print(worst.head(20).select(["rank", "dura_ns"]).with_columns((pl.col("dura_ns")/1e6).alias("dura_ms")))
print("\nFastest 20 ranks:")
print(worst.tail(20).select(["rank", "dura_ns"]).with_columns((pl.col("dura_ns")/1e6).alias("dura_ms")))
EOF

**Root cause identified!**

`RedistributeAndRefineMeshBlocks` has 5x variance (20ms vs 110ms). The slow ranks match the MPI_Allreduce stragglers exactly (ranks 700, 2772, 3057, 3058, 1252).

This is **AMR load imbalance** - some ranks receive more mesh blocks to process during redistribution.

input.command:
python3 << 'EOF'
import polars as pl
from collections import Counter

parquet_root = "/mnt/ltio/orcajobs/suites/20260108/amr-agg4-r4096-n2000-run1/20_or_disable_paused/parquet"

ks = pl.scan_parquet(f"{parquet_root}/kokkos_slow/**/*.parquet").collect()
redistribute = ks.filter(pl.col("probe_name").str.contains("RedistributeAndRefine"))

# For each timestep, find top 10 slowest ranks
slow_ranks_all = []
for ts in redistribute["timestep"].unique().to_list():
    ts_data = redistribute.filter(pl.col("timestep") == ts).sort("dura_ns", descending=True)
    slow_ranks = ts_data.head(10)["rank"].to_list()
    slow_ranks_all.extend(slow_ranks)

# Count frequency of being in top 10 slowest
rank_freq = Counter(slow_ranks_all)
print(f"=== Ranks that frequently appear in top 10 slowest ===")
print(f"Total timesteps: {redistribute['timestep'].n_unique()}")
print(f"Unique ranks in top-10 slow: {len(rank_freq)}")
print("\nMost frequent slow ranks:")
for rank, count in rank_freq.most_common(30):
    node = rank // 32
    print(f"  Rank {rank:4d} (node {node:3d}): {count}/{redistribute['timestep'].n_unique()} timesteps")

# Are slow ranks clustered by node?
print("\n=== Node clustering of frequently slow ranks ===")
top_slow_ranks = [r for r, c in rank_freq.most_common(50)]
slow_nodes = [r // 32 for r in top_slow_ranks]
node_freq = Counter(slow_nodes)
print("Nodes with multiple slow ranks:")
for node, cnt in node_freq.most_common(10):
    if cnt > 1:
        ranks_on_node = [r for r in top_slow_ranks if r // 32 == node]
        print(f"  Node {node:3d}: {cnt} ranks - {ranks_on_node}")
EOF

1. Primary Request and Intent:
   The user is conducting a user study with the ORCA observability framework to debug a performance anomaly in an AMR (Adaptive Mesh Refinement) code (Sedov Blast Wave 3D with 4096 MPI ranks). The task involves:
   - Learning the ORCA framework through warm-up exercises
   - Using OrcaFlow (YAML-based SQL programming layer) to collect and filter telemetry data efficiently
   - Identifying and diagnosing random straggler behavior causing collective wait times
   - Understanding the root cause by correlating MPI collective durations with kokkos kernel events

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - ORCA: Multi-tier observability framework for MPI/BSP applications
   - OrcaFlow: YAML-based data flow definition with SQL support
   - TS2PC: Two-phase commit protocol for coordinated pause/resume across ranks
   - `swid` (sync window ID): Monotonic identifier for collective calls
   - `timestep`: BSP timestep, unit of batching/analysis
   - Stragglers: Ranks that arrive late to collectives (identified by LOW collective duration)
   - AMR (Adaptive Mesh Refinement): Dynamic mesh operations causing load imbalance
   - Commands: `cmdrunner_main "status|resume|pause|set-flow file|disable-probe"`

3. Files and Code Sections:
   - **`/users/ankushj/repos/orca-workspace/orca-utils/orca-scripts/flows/agentic_workflow.md`**

     - Initial flow for counting events per probe
     ```yaml
     nodes:
       mpi_coll_counts:
         from: [mpi_collectives]
         sql: |
           SELECT timestep as ts, swid, probe_name, COUNT(*) as cnt
           FROM mpi_collectives GROUP BY timestep, swid, probe_name
       kokkos_counts:
         from: [kokkos_events]
         sql: |
           SELECT timestep as ts, swid, probe_name, COUNT(*) as cnt
           FROM kokkos_events GROUP BY timestep, swid, probe_name
       mpi_msg_counts:
         from: [mpi_messages]
         sql: |
           SELECT timestep as ts, swid, probe_name, COUNT(*) as cnt
           FROM mpi_messages GROUP BY timestep, swid, probe_name
     sinks:
       mpi_coll_counts_pq: {from: mpi_coll_counts, type: parquet}
       kokkos_counts_pq: {from: kokkos_counts, type: parquet}
       mpi_msg_counts_pq: {from: mpi_msg_counts, type: parquet}
     ```

content.thinking:
Now I have a comprehensive understanding of the AMR load balancing code. The key insights are:

1. **Root Cause of Variance**: The `RedistributeAndRefineMeshBlocks` function has multiple steps that depend on:
   - Number of blocks being sent/received (`nsend`, `nrecv`)
   - Whether blocks are at the same level, or being refined (c2f) or coarsened (f2c)
   - MPI communication patterns (receiving data from different ranks)

2. **Load Balancing Strategy** (in `AssignBlocks`):
   - Tries to distribute blocks by equal cost from the end of the block list
   - Block assignment is index-contiguous (space-filling curve)
   - Attempts to give each rank equal total cost

3. **Key Steps in Redistribution**:
   - Step 1: Construct new block lists (mapping old to new)
   - Step 2: Calculate new load balance (calls `amr::lb::LoadBalance::AssignBlocksMpi`)
   - Steps 3-7: Count/allocate/pack send/receive buffers
   - Step 8: Construct new MeshBlock list (local data movement)
   - Step 9: Receive data and unpack

4. **Why some ranks take longer**:
   - Ranks that receive more blocks from other ranks have more MPI communication
   - Ranks that handle fine-to-coarse or coarse-to-fine transitions do restriction/prolongation operations
   - The waiting for MPI receives in Step 9 causes synchronization delays

Let me now summarize my findings for the user. The root cause analysis is complete.

content.text:
Now I have a complete picture. Let me summarize the root cause analysis.

## Root Cause Analysis Summary

Based on the investigation of the AMR performance anomaly in the 4096-rank Sedov Blast Wave simulation, here's what I found:

### The Problem
MPI_Allreduce collectives at swid_offset 6-7 within timesteps show a **95ms duration range** - some ranks wait 100ms while others take only 5ms. The stragglers are **random** (185 unique ranks across 195 straggler appearances).

### Root Cause: AMR Load Imbalance in `RedistributeAndRefineMeshBlocks`

The correlated kokkos events reveal that **`RedistributeAndRefineMeshBlocks`** has 5x execution time variance (20ms-110ms) across ranks. This function in `amr_loadbalance.cpp:502-980` performs:

1. **Step 1-2**: Calculate new load balance using cost-based block assignment
2. **Steps 3-7**: Count, allocate, pack, and send mesh block data via MPI
3. **Step 8**: Construct new MeshBlock list with data movement
4. **Step 9**: Receive and unpack data from other ranks

### Why Variance Occurs

The load balancing algorithm (`AssignBlocks` at line 123) tries to place equal-cost blocks contiguously, but:

1. **Communication Asymmetry**: Ranks receiving more blocks from remote ranks have more MPI communication overhead
2. **Refinement Type**: Fine-to-coarse (f2c) and coarse-to-fine (c2f) transitions require restriction/prolongation operations that are more expensive than same-level transfers
3. **MPI Wait Time**: Step 9 does `MPI_Test` polling, causing ranks to wait for data from slower senders

### Affected Collectives

The straggler effect appears at `swid_offset 6-7` because:
- The AMR load balancing happens periodically
- The `MPI_Allreduce` that follows `RedistributeAndRefineMeshBlocks` becomes the synchronization point
- Ranks completing redistribution early wait for slower ranks

### Recommendation

The imbalance is inherent to AMR workloads where mesh refinement changes require data redistribution. The variance correlates with how much mesh data each rank needs to send/receive during rebalancing, which varies based on the physical problem evolution. Potential mitigations would involve:
- Adjusting the load balancing interval (`lb_interval_`)
- Tuning the load balancing policy (line 187: `Globals::lb_policy`)
- Investigating why certain ranks consistently receive more incoming blocks during redistribution

content.thinking:
Interesting! I see:
- `RedistributeAndRefineMeshBlocks` has max 112ms, avg 44ms - but only 131072 events (which is 32 timesteps Г— 4096 ranks)
- The internal Steps (7, 7b, 9) have very few events captured (only 2-8 events)

This suggests the sub-steps are generally not taking > 10ms threshold I set. Let me check if those steps have variance by looking at ALL kokkos events for those specific probes, not just the slow ones (>10ms).

Actually wait - I should analyze this differently. Let me:
1. Look at the ranks that have slow `RedistributeAndRefineMeshBlocks` calls
2. See if those same ranks also have slow sub-steps

But first, let me get a better picture of the sub-step timing. The fact that only 2-8 events for the substeps exceeded 10ms means most of the time is NOT in those individually instrumented steps. Let me look at what's happening.

Actually, the hierarchy is:
- LoadBalancingAndAdaptiveMeshRefinement (parent)
  - UpdateMeshBlockTree
  - RedistributeAndRefineMeshBlocks
    - Step 1-9 (sub-regions)
  - Mesh::Initialize

Let me look at the timing more carefully. If RedistributeAndRefineMeshBlocks takes 44ms avg and 112ms max, but its sub-steps (7, 7b, 9) only have 2-8 events > 10ms, then maybe there are other steps that are slow, or maybe the time is in code that's not in any sub-region.

Let me look at the full kokkos data (not just slow) to understand the timing breakdown better. But that might be too much data. Let me instead focus on a specific timestep with anomalies and look at all the kokkos events for ranks that are slow vs fast on that timestep.

content.text: The sub-steps (Step 7, 7b, 9) have very few events exceeding 10ms threshold. Let me lower the threshold and look at the timing breakdown more carefully for the redistributionfunction.

input.command:
cd /mnt/ltio/orcajobs/suites/20260108/amr-agg4-r4096-n2000-run1/20_or_disable_paused/parquet && python3 -c "
import polars as pl

# Load slow kokkos events
kokkos = pl.read_parquet('kokkos_slow/**/*.parquet')

# Focus on RedistributeAndRefineMeshBlocks - look at the variance per timestep
rnr = kokkos.filter(pl.col('probe_name').str.contains('RedistributeAndRefine'))

print('RedistributeAndRefineMeshBlocks timing per timestep:')
ts_stats = rnr.group_by('timestep').agg(
    pl.len().alias('cnt'),
    (pl.col('dura_ns').min() / 1e6).alias('min_ms'),
    (pl.col('dura_ns').mean() / 1e6).alias('avg_ms'),
    (pl.col('dura_ns').max() / 1e6).alias('max_ms'),
    ((pl.col('dura_ns').max() - pl.col('dura_ns').min()) / 1e6).alias('range_ms')
).sort('timestep')
print(ts_stats.head(20))

# Pick a timestep with high variance
print()
print('Timesteps with highest range:')
print(ts_stats.sort('range_ms', descending=True).head(10))
"

content.thinking:
Interesting! Some timesteps have ~90ms range (max 110ms, min 20ms), while others have only ~40ms range (max 60ms, min 20ms). This is a bimodal pattern - there's something happening on certain timesteps that causes extra variance.

Let me look at the sub-steps for a high-variance timestep (like ts=343) to see where the extra 50ms is going.

content.text: There's a bimodal pattern - some timesteps have ~90ms range while others have ~40ms. Let me look at the sub-step breakdown for a high-variance timestep (343) vs a low-variance one (354).

input.command:
cd /mnt/ltio/orcajobs/suites/20260108/amr-agg4-r4096-n2000-run1/20_or_disable_paused/parquet && python3 -c "
import polars as pl

# Load slow kokkos events
kokkos = pl.read_parquet('kokkos_slow/**/*.parquet')

# Find the slow ranks on ts=343
rnr_343 = kokkos.filter(
    (pl.col('timestep') == 343) & 
    pl.col('probe_name').str.contains('RedistributeAndRefine')
)

# Get the slowest and fastest ranks
slowest = rnr_343.sort('dura_ns', descending=True).head(10)
fastest = rnr_343.sort('dura_ns').head(10)

print('Slowest ranks on ts=343 (RedistributeAndRefineMeshBlocks):')
print(slowest.select(['rank', 'dura_ns']).with_columns((pl.col('dura_ns')/1e6).alias('ms')))

print()
print('Fastest ranks on ts=343:')
print(fastest.select(['rank', 'dura_ns']).with_columns((pl.col('dura_ns')/1e6).alias('ms')))

# Now look at ALL kokkos events for these ranks on ts=343 to see sub-step breakdown
slow_ranks = slowest['rank'].to_list()[:5]
fast_ranks = fastest['rank'].to_list()[:5]

print()
print(f'Slow ranks to investigate: {slow_ranks}')
print(f'Fast ranks to compare: {fast_ranks}')
"

content.thinking:
Now I need to look at the sub-regions for these ranks. But the slow kokkos only captured events > 10ms. I need to modify my flow to capture all events for the RedistributeAndRefineMeshBlocks sub-steps. Actually, wait - let me think about this differently.

The sub-steps are nested inside RedistributeAndRefineMeshBlocks. If the parent takes 110ms on slow ranks vs 20ms on fast ranks, but I only have 2-8 events for the sub-steps exceeding 10ms, then either:
1. The time is distributed across many sub-steps (each < 10ms)
2. The time is in code not covered by any profiling region

Actually, looking at the code again, I see there are nested regions. The sub-regions (Steps 1-9) should have their own timing. But my filter of >10ms might be cutting out important data.

Let me create a new flow that captures more detail for the redistribution function specifically.

Actually, wait - the user said "do you have an accounting of whether that time is captured by steps 1-9?" The point is that if the slow ranks take 90ms extra, that extra time should show up in one of the Steps. If it doesn't, then either:
1. The instrumentation is incomplete
2. Something else is happening (like the extra time is at the end after step 9)

Let me check if the slow kokkos data has ANY sub-step events for the slow ranks.

content.text: Let me check if we captured any sub-step events for these slow ranks to see where the extra 90ms goes.

input.command:
cd /mnt/ltio/orcajobs/suites/20260108/amr-agg4-r4096-n2000-run1/20_or_disable_paused/parquet && python3 -c "
import polars as pl

# Load slow kokkos events
kokkos = pl.read_parquet('kokkos_slow/**/*.parquet')

# Look at ALL events for the slow ranks on ts=343
slow_ranks = [700, 2772, 3057, 3058, 1252]
fast_ranks = [506, 1387, 2279, 942, 938]

ts343_slow = kokkos.filter(
    (pl.col('timestep') == 343) & 
    pl.col('rank').is_in(slow_ranks)
)

ts343_fast = kokkos.filter(
    (pl.col('timestep') == 343) & 
    pl.col('rank').is_in(fast_ranks)
)

print('All slow kokkos events for SLOW ranks on ts=343:')
print(ts343_slow.group_by('probe_name').agg(
    pl.len().alias('cnt'),
    (pl.col('dura_ns').sum() / 1e6).alias('total_ms'),
    (pl.col('dura_ns').mean() / 1e6).alias('avg_ms')
).sort('total_ms', descending=True))

print()
print('All slow kokkos events for FAST ranks on ts=343:')
print(ts343_fast.group_by('probe_name').agg(
    pl.len().alias('cnt'),
    (pl.col('dura_ns').sum() / 1e6).alias('total_ms'),
    (pl.col('dura_ns').mean() / 1e6).alias('avg_ms')
).sort('total_ms', descending=True))
"

content.thinking:
Now I see the breakdown! On slow ranks:
- RedistributeAndRefineMeshBlocks: 110ms avg
- Mesh::Initialize: 97ms avg (this is the big one!)
- Mesh::Initialize::BuildBoundaryBuffers: 72ms (only 1 rank had this > 10ms)- Mesh::Initialize::Send: 18ms avg

On fast ranks:
- RedistributeAndRefineMeshBlocks: 20ms avg
- Mesh::Initialize: 14ms avg
- UpdateMeshBlockTree: 17ms avgSo the main difference is in `Mesh::Initialize` - 97ms on slow ranks vs 14ms on fast ranks. That's 83ms difference which accounts for most of the 90ms range.

But we're missing the sub-step events for fast ranks (no Initialize::BuildBoundaryBuffers, no Initialize::Send) because they're under 10ms. And we're also missing the Step 1-9 regions entirely - they must all be < 10ms.

Let me check what's inside Mesh::Initialize to understand what's causing the 83ms difference. The user is saying this is a "bug" like node throttling - so maybe certain nodes consistently have slow Mesh::Initialize.

Let me check if the slow ranks are on specific nodes. With 4096 ranks, there are probably 64 ranks per node (or some other power of 2).

content.text: The difference is in `Mesh::Initialize` - 97ms on slow ranks vs 14ms on fast ranks. That's an 83ms gap. But we're missing sub-step detail. Let me check if the slow ranks cluster on specific nodes.

input.command:
cd /mnt/ltio/orcajobs/suites/20260108/amr-agg4-r4096-n2000-run1/20_or_disable_paused/parquet && python3 -c "
import polars as pl

# Load slow kokkos events  
kokkos = pl.read_parquet('kokkos_slow/**/*.parquet')

# Get all RedistributeAndRefineMeshBlocks events
rnr = kokkos.filter(pl.col('probe_name').str.contains('RedistributeAndRefine'))

# Assuming 64 ranks per node (common for HPC)
ranks_per_node = 64

# Add node column
rnr = rnr.with_columns((pl.col('rank') // ranks_per_node).alias('node'))

# Find which ranks are consistently slow (> 100ms)
slow_events = rnr.filter(pl.col('dura_ns') > 100_000_000)  # > 100ms

print('Ranks with RedistributeAndRefine > 100ms:')
rank_counts = slow_events.group_by('rank').agg(pl.len().alias('slow_count')).sort('slow_count', descending=True)
print(rank_counts.head(20))

print()
print('Nodes with most slow events:')
node_counts = slow_events.group_by('node').agg(pl.len().alias('slow_count')).sort('slow_count', descending=True)
print(node_counts.head(20))

print()
print(f'Total slow events: {len(slow_events)}')
print(f'Unique ranks with slow events: {slow_events[\"rank\"].n_unique()}')
print(f'Unique nodes with slow events: {slow_events[\"node\"].n_unique()}')
"

input.command:
cd /mnt/ltio/orcajobs/suites/20260108/amr-agg4-r4096-n2000-run1/20_or_disable_paused/parquet && python3 -c "
import polars as pl

# Load slow kokkos events  
kokkos = pl.read_parquet('kokkos_slow/**/*.parquet')

# Get all RedistributeAndRefineMeshBlocks events
rnr = kokkos.filter(pl.col('probe_name').str.contains('RedistributeAndRefine'))

# How many timesteps total?
n_ts = rnr['timestep'].n_unique()
print(f'Total timesteps with RedistributeAndRefine: {n_ts}')

# Look at average duration per rank across all timesteps
rank_stats = rnr.group_by('rank').agg(
    pl.len().alias('cnt'),
    (pl.col('dura_ns').mean() / 1e6).alias('avg_ms'),
    (pl.col('dura_ns').std() / 1e6).alias('std_ms')
).sort('avg_ms', descending=True)

print()
print('Ranks with highest AVERAGE RedistributeAndRefine time:')
print(rank_stats.head(20))

print()
print('Ranks with lowest AVERAGE time:')
print(rank_stats.sort('avg_ms').head(20))

# Are the slow ranks consistently slow?
print()
print('Distribution of avg times:')
print(rank_stats['avg_ms'].describe())
"

import polars as pl

kokkos = pl.read_parquet('kokkos_slow/**/*.parquet')
rnr = kokkos.filter(pl.col('probe_name').str.contains('RedistributeAndRefine'))

# Compute per-timestep stats
ts_stats = rnr.group_by('timestep').agg(
    (pl.col('dura_ns').max() / 1e6).alias('max_ms'),
    (pl.col('dura_ns').min() / 1e6).alias('min_ms'),
    ((pl.col('dura_ns').max() - pl.col('dura_ns').min()) / 1e6).alias('range_ms')
).sort('timestep')

# Classify timesteps
high_var_ts = ts_stats.filter(pl.col('range_ms') > 80)['timestep'].to_list()
low_var_ts = ts_stats.filter(pl.col('range_ms') < 50)['timestep'].to_list()

print(f'High variance timesteps (range > 80ms): {high_var_ts}')
print(f'Low variance timesteps (range < 50ms): {low_var_ts}')

# Look at the pattern
print()
print('Timestep sequence and variance:')
for row in ts_stats.iter_rows(named=True):
    marker = '*** HIGH ***' if row['range_ms'] > 80 else ''
    print(f\"ts={row['timestep']}: range={row['range_ms']:.1f}ms {marker}\")
"

rnr = kokkos.filter(pl.col('probe_name').str.contains('RedistributeAndRefine'))

content.thinking:
Interesting! On ts=343:- Rank 700: Mesh::Initialize = 93ms, with BuildBoundaryBuffers = 72ms (accounts for most of it!)
- Rank 2772: Mesh::Initialize = 101ms, Send = 20ms (only 20% accounted for)
- Rank 3057: Mesh::Initialize = 99ms, Send = 16ms (only 16% accounted for)For rank 700, we captured `BuildBoundaryBuffers` which is 72ms out of 93ms - that's where the time goes!
For ranks 2772 and 3057, the Send is only 15-20ms, so where's the other 80ms?

The sub-regions are:
- PreComm- SetupPersistentMPI
- BuildAndPost (BuildBoundaryBuffers + StartReceiveBoundaryBuffers)- SendThen the receive loop (while !all_received) which is NOT instrumented!

So the likely culprit is either:1. BuildBoundaryBuffers (captured for rank 700 at 72ms)2. The receive loop (not captured, but waiting for MPI)

Let me check if there are more sub-region events we're missing. Also, only 21 events captured for BuildBoundaryBuffers but 131K for Mesh::Initialize - many ranks don't have that sub-region captured because it's < 10ms.

Let me lower the filter threshold or collect full kokkos data to get better accounting.

content.text: Only 21 events for `BuildBoundaryBuffers` but 131K for `Mesh::Initialize` - most sub-steps are under 10ms threshold. The receive loop after `Send` isn't instrumented. Let me create a more detailed flow to capture all events in the AMR regions.

input.content:
nodes:
  # Capture all kokkos events related to AMR/Initialize with lower threshold (1ms)
  kokkos_amr:
    from: [kokkos_events]
    sql: |
      SELECT timestep, swid, rank, probe_name, dura_ns
      FROM kokkos_events
      WHERE dura_ns > 1000000
        AND (probe_name LIKE '%Redistribute%'
             OR probe_name LIKE '%Initialize%'
             OR probe_name LIKE '%LoadBalancing%'
             OR probe_name LIKE '%Step %')

sinks:
  kokkos_amr_pq:
    from: kokkos_amr
    type: parquet
  mpisync_pq:
    from: mpi_collectives
    type: parquet

content.thinking:
Now I have more detail. Let me find a high-variance timestep and look at the sub-step breakdown for slow vs fast ranks. 

Looking at the numbers:- RedistributeAndRefineMeshBlocks: 47ms avg, 113ms max- Mesh::Initialize: 38ms avg, 107ms max - Step 8 (Construct new MeshBlockList): 4.6ms avg, 16ms max- Step 9 (Recv data and unpack): 2.2ms avg, 14ms max- Step 7: 3.3ms avg, 12ms max

The sub-steps (7, 8, 9) are relatively small. The bulk of the time seems to be in:
1. RedistributeAndRefine (~47ms)
2. Mesh::Initialize (~38ms)
3. Initialize::Send (~14ms)

But wait - Initialize::Send is 14ms avg, and Initialize is 38ms avg. So there's 24ms unaccounted for in Initialize alone. Let me look at a specific timestep to understand where the 90ms variance goes.

content.text: Now let me look at the timing breakdown for a high-variance timestep.

input.command:
cd /mnt/ltio/orcajobs/suites/20260108/amr-agg4-r4096-n2000-run1/20_or_disable_paused/parquet && python3 -c "
import polars as pl

kokkos = pl.read_parquet('kokkos_amr/**/*.parquet')

# Find RedistributeAndRefineMeshBlocks variance per timestep
rnr = kokkos.filter(pl.col('probe_name').str.contains('RedistributeAndRefine'))

ts_stats = rnr.group_by('timestep').agg(
    (pl.col('dura_ns').min() / 1e6).alias('min_ms'),
    (pl.col('dura_ns').max() / 1e6).alias('max_ms'),
    ((pl.col('dura_ns').max() - pl.col('dura_ns').min()) / 1e6).alias('range_ms')
).sort('range_ms', descending=True)

print('Timesteps with highest variance in RedistributeAndRefineMeshBlocks:')
print(ts_stats.head(10))

# Pick a high-variance timestep
high_var_ts = ts_stats.head(1)['timestep'][0]
print(f'\\nAnalyzing timestep {high_var_ts}')
"

input.command:
cd /mnt/ltio/orcajobs/suites/20260108/amr-agg4-r4096-n2000-run1/20_or_disable_paused/parquet && python3 -c "
import polars as pl

kokkos = pl.read_parquet('kokkos_amr/**/*.parquet')

# Focus on ts=353
ts353 = kokkos.filter(pl.col('timestep') == 353)

# Find slow and fast ranks based on RedistributeAndRefineMeshBlocks
rnr = ts353.filter(pl.col('probe_name').str.contains('RedistributeAndRefine'))

slowest_ranks = rnr.sort('dura_ns', descending=True).head(5)['rank'].to_list()
fastest_ranks = rnr.sort('dura_ns').head(5)['rank'].to_list()

print(f'Slowest ranks: {slowest_ranks}')
print(f'Fastest ranks: {fastest_ranks}')

# Get all events for these ranks
slow_events = ts353.filter(pl.col('rank').is_in(slowest_ranks))
fast_events = ts353.filter(pl.col('rank').is_in(fastest_ranks))

print()
print('=== SLOW RANKS sub-step breakdown ===')
slow_breakdown = slow_events.group_by('probe_name').agg(
    pl.len().alias('cnt'),
    (pl.col('dura_ns').sum() / 1e6).alias('total_ms'),
    (pl.col('dura_ns').mean() / 1e6).alias('avg_ms')
).sort('total_ms', descending=True)
print(slow_breakdown)

print()
print('=== FAST RANKS sub-step breakdown ===')
fast_breakdown = fast_events.group_by('probe_name').agg(
    pl.len().alias('cnt'),
    (pl.col('dura_ns').sum() / 1e6).alias('total_ms'),
    (pl.col('dura_ns').mean() / 1e6).alias('avg_ms')
).sort('total_ms', descending=True)
print(fast_breakdown)
"

**Slow ranks (avg per rank):**
- RedistributeAndRefine: 111ms
- Mesh::Initialize: 100ms
- Initialize::Send: 21ms

- Step 8: 5.9ms

**Fast ranks (avg per rank):**
- RedistributeAndRefine: 19ms
- Mesh::Initialize: 15ms
- Initialize::Send: 2.7ms
- Step 8: 3.9ms

Key differences:
1. **Initialize::Send**: 21ms vs 2.7ms (8x slower)
2. **Initialize::BuildBoundaryBuffers**: 39ms on 2 slow ranks vs not captured on fast ranks
3. **Mesh::Initialize overall**: 100ms vs 15ms (7x slower)

Now let me see if I can account for the time in Initialize:
- Slow ranks: Initialize=100ms, Send=21ms, BuildBoundaryBuffers=39ms вҶ’ That's 60ms accounted for, 40ms unaccounted
- Fast ranks: Initialize=15ms, Send=2.7ms вҶ’ Only 2.7ms accounted for, 12ms unaccounted

The unaccounted time in Initialize is likely in the receive loop (not instrumented) where ranks wait for MPI data from others. The slow ranks spend more time receiving because they need data from remote ranks that are slow senders.

cd /mnt/ltio/orcajobs/suites/20260108/amr-agg4-r4096-n2000-run1/20_or_disable_paused/parquet && python3 -c "
import polars as pl

kokkos = pl.read_parquet('kokkos_amr/**/*.parquet')

# Get RedistributeAndRefineMeshBlocks times for all ranks across all timesteps
rnr = kokkos.filter(pl.col('probe_name').str.contains('RedistributeAndRefine'))

# Calculate average time per rank  
rank_avg = rnr.group_by('rank').agg(
    (pl.col('dura_ns').mean() / 1e6).alias('avg_ms')
)

# Try different ranks-per-node values
for rpn in [56, 64, 128]:
    rank_avg_with_node = rank_avg.with_columns(
        (pl.col('rank') // rpn).alias('node'),
        (pl.col('rank') % rpn).alias('rank_in_node')
    )
    
    # Compute node-level stats
    node_stats = rank_avg_with_node.group_by('node').agg(
        pl.col('avg_ms').mean().alias('node_avg_ms'),
        pl.col('avg_ms').std().alias('node_std_ms')
    ).sort('node_avg_ms', descending=True)
    
    print(f'\\n=== rpn={rpn} ===')
    print(f'Slowest nodes: {node_stats.head(5)[\"node\"].to_list()}')
    print(f'Fastest nodes: {node_stats.sort(\"node_avg_ms\").head(5)[\"node\"].to_list()}')
    print(f'Node avg variance: {node_stats[\"node_avg_ms\"].std():.2f}ms')
    
    # Check if certain ranks-within-node are consistently slow
    rank_in_node_stats = rank_avg_with_node.group_by('rank_in_node').agg(
        pl.col('avg_ms').mean().alias('avg_ms')
    ).sort('avg_ms', descending=True)
    print(f'Slowest rank-in-node positions: {rank_in_node_stats.head(5)[\"rank_in_node\"].to_list()}')
    print(f'Fastest rank-in-node positions: {rank_in_node_stats.sort(\"avg_ms\").head(5)[\"rank_in_node\"].to_list()}')
"

cd /mnt/ltio/orcajobs/suites/20260108/amr-agg4-r4096-n2000-run1/20_or_disable_paused/parquet && python3 -c "
import polars as pl

kokkos = pl.read_parquet('kokkos_amr/**/*.parquet')

# Get RedistributeAndRefineMeshBlocks times
rnr = kokkos.filter(pl.col('probe_name').str.contains('RedistributeAndRefine'))

# With 128 ranks per node
rpn = 128

# Add node and rank-in-node columns
rnr = rnr.with_columns(
    (pl.col('rank') // rpn).alias('node'),
    (pl.col('rank') % rpn).alias('rank_in_node')
)

# Compute average time by rank-in-node position
pos_avg = rnr.group_by('rank_in_node').agg(
    (pl.col('dura_ns').mean() / 1e6).alias('avg_ms'),
    (pl.col('dura_ns').std() / 1e6).alias('std_ms')
).sort('rank_in_node')

# Print the pattern
print('Average time by rank-in-node position (assuming 128 ranks/node):')
print('Position range | Avg (ms) | Std (ms)')
print('-' * 40)

# Group by ranges of 8 to see socket/core patterns
for start in range(0, 128, 8):
    end = start + 8
    subset = pos_avg.filter((pl.col('rank_in_node') >= start) & (pl.col('rank_in_node') < end))
    avg = subset['avg_ms'].mean()
    std = subset['std_ms'].mean()
    marker = '***' if avg > 50 else ''
    print(f'{start:3d}-{end-1:3d}        | {avg:6.1f}   | {std:5.1f}  {marker}')
"

content.text: No clear position pattern. Let me check if on specific high-variance timesteps, the slow ranks cluster on specific nodes.

input.command:
cd /mnt/ltio/orcajobs/suites/20260108/amr-agg4-r4096-n2000-run1/20_or_disable_paused/parquet && python3 -c "
import polars as pl

kokkos = pl.read_parquet('kokkos_amr/**/*.parquet')
rnr = kokkos.filter(pl.col('probe_name').str.contains('RedistributeAndRefine'))

# High variance timesteps
high_var_ts = [353, 343, 320, 288]

# For each timestep, find slow ranks and see if they cluster
rpn = 64  # ranks per node

for ts in high_var_ts:
    ts_data = rnr.filter(pl.col('timestep') == ts)
    
    # Top 20 slowest ranks
    slowest = ts_data.sort('dura_ns', descending=True).head(20)
    slow_ranks = slowest['rank'].to_list()
    slow_nodes = [r // rpn for r in slow_ranks]
    
    # Count by node
    from collections import Counter
    node_counts = Counter(slow_nodes)
    
    max_node = node_counts.most_common(1)[0] if node_counts else (None, 0)
    max_time = slowest['dura_ns'].max() / 1e6
    min_time = slowest['dura_ns'].min() / 1e6
    
    print(f'ts={ts}: slow_ranks on nodes: {dict(node_counts.most_common(5))}')
    print(f'  Max node has {max_node[1]}/20 slow ranks (node {max_node[0]})')
    print(f'  Time range of top 20: {min_time:.1f} - {max_time:.1f} ms')
    print()
"

content.thinking:
Interesting! There's some node clustering:- ts=320: node 32 has 5 out of 20 slowest ranks- ts=288: node 51 has 4 out of 20 slowest ranks
- ts=353: node 31 has 3 out of 20 slowest ranks- ts=343: node 38 has 3 out of 20 slowest ranksWith 64 nodes total and random distribution, we'd expect ~0.3 ranks per node in the top 20. So having 3-5 is significantly above chance. But it's not like ALL 20 are on one node.Let me look more carefully at ts=320 where node 32 has 5 of the slowest ranks.

content.text: Node 32 has 5/20 slowest ranks on ts=320 - that's significant. Let me dig deeper.

input.command:
cd /mnt/ltio/orcajobs/suites/20260108/amr-agg4-r4096-n2000-run1/20_or_disable_paused/parquet && python3 -c "
import polars as pl

kokkos = pl.read_parquet('kokkos_amr/**/*.parquet')
rnr = kokkos.filter(pl.col('probe_name').str.contains('RedistributeAndRefine'))

rpn = 64

# Focus on ts=320 and node 32
ts320 = rnr.filter(pl.col('timestep') == 320)
ts320 = ts320.with_columns((pl.col('rank') // rpn).alias('node'))

# Get all ranks on node 32
node32_ts320 = ts320.filter(pl.col('node') == 32)
print(f'Node 32 has {len(node32_ts320)} ranks on ts=320')
print(f'  Min time: {node32_ts320[\"dura_ns\"].min()/1e6:.1f} ms')
print(f'  Max time: {node32_ts320[\"dura_ns\"].max()/1e6:.1f} ms')
print(f'  Avg time: {node32_ts320[\"dura_ns\"].mean()/1e6:.1f} ms')

# Compare to other nodes
node_stats = ts320.group_by('node').agg(
    (pl.col('dura_ns').mean() / 1e6).alias('avg_ms'),
    (pl.col('dura_ns').max() / 1e6).alias('max_ms')
).sort('avg_ms', descending=True)

print()
print('Top 10 slowest nodes on ts=320:')
print(node_stats.head(10))

print()
print('Top 10 fastest nodes:')
print(node_stats.sort('avg_ms').head(10))
"

content.thinking:
Now I see something interesting! The difference between slowest and fastest nodes:
- Slowest node avg: ~57ms
- Fastest node avg: ~40ms- Difference: 17ms at node level

But within nodes there's huge variance:
- Node 32: min=31ms, max=112ms (81ms range within the same node!)

So the variance is NOT primarily at the node level. There's something else going on.

Wait - looking at the max times:
- Node 7 (fastest avg=40ms): max=59ms - tight range
- Node 32 (avg=56ms): max=112ms - huge range
- Node 55 (slowest avg=57ms): max=107ms - huge range

The fast nodes have tight distributions (max close to avg), while slow nodes have wide distributions. This suggests it's not a uniform node-level slowdown like overheating would cause.

Let me look at this from a different angle. The user mentioned this could be like "node throttling" - but what if it's a **single slow core** on certain nodes that's causing some ranks to be slow while others on the same node are fine?

content.text: Within node 32, there's 31-112ms range! The variance is NOT at the node level. Let me check if specific cores/ranks within nodes are consistently slow.

input.command:
cd /mnt/ltio/orcajobs/suites/20260108/amr-agg4-r4096-n2000-run1/20_or_disable_paused/parquet && python3 -c "
import polars as pl

kokkos = pl.read_parquet('kokkos_amr/**/*.parquet')
rnr = kokkos.filter(pl.col('probe_name').str.contains('RedistributeAndRefine'))

rpn = 64

# Add node and position-in-node
rnr = rnr.with_columns(
    (pl.col('rank') // rpn).alias('node'),
    (pl.col('rank') % rpn).alias('pos_in_node')
)

# For each node, find which positions are consistently slow
# Look at average time by (node, pos_in_node) across all timesteps
node_pos_avg = rnr.group_by(['node', 'pos_in_node']).agg(
    (pl.col('dura_ns').mean() / 1e6).alias('avg_ms'),
    pl.col('rank').first().alias('rank')
)

# Find the slowest positions per node
slowest_per_node = node_pos_avg.sort(['node', 'avg_ms'], descending=[False, True]).group_by('node').head(3)

# Find nodes where positions have extreme variance
node_variance = node_pos_avg.group_by('node').agg(
    pl.col('avg_ms').std().alias('pos_std'),
    pl.col('avg_ms').mean().alias('pos_mean'),
    pl.col('avg_ms').max().alias('pos_max'),
    pl.col('avg_ms').min().alias('pos_min')
).with_columns(
    (pl.col('pos_max') - pl.col('pos_min')).alias('pos_range')
).sort('pos_range', descending=True)

print('Nodes with highest intra-node variance:')
print(node_variance.head(15))

print()
# Check the top nodes with high intra-node variance
top_var_nodes = node_variance.head(3)['node'].to_list()
for node in top_var_nodes:
    node_data = node_pos_avg.filter(pl.col('node') == node).sort('avg_ms', descending=True)
    print(f'\\nNode {node} - positions by avg time:')
    print(f'  Slowest 5: {list(zip(node_data.head(5)[\"pos_in_node\"].to_list(), [f\"{x:.1f}ms\" for x in node_data.head(5)[\"avg_ms\"].to_list()]))}')
    print(f'  Fastest 5: {list(zip(node_data.tail(5)[\"pos_in_node\"].to_list(), [f\"{x:.1f}ms\" for x in node_data.tail(5)[\"avg_ms\"].to_list()]))}')
"
